title.title: Bitcoin: The First Permissionless Blockchain
title.subtitle: Architecture, transactions, Proof of Work, and the Blockchain Trilemma

lightning.heading: Lightning Network — The Vault Model
lightning.introB: on Bitcoin. Inside the vault they trade unlimited times instantly, off-chain. Closing the vault is the

steps.idle: Channel closed. Click Open Channel to begin.
steps.opening: 1. On-chain OPEN tx — Alice and Bob each lock 1 BTC in a 2-of-2 vault
steps.open: Channel OPEN — vault holds 2 BTC. Off-chain payments are now free and instant.
steps.closing: 2. On-chain CLOSE tx — vault releases final balances back to Alice and Bob

lightning.payStep: ⚡ Off-chain payment — {{dir}} {{amount}} BTC (no Bitcoin tx, channel state updates)
lightning.settledStep: ✓ Settled — only 2 on-chain txs total: OPEN + CLOSE. {{count}} off-chain payments cost ~$0.

svg.l1Sub: Two on-chain txs total: OPEN + CLOSE

panel.noPayments: Open the channel and start paying.
panel.trickBody: the vault holds the funds. Every payment is a signed agreement on how the vault's contents should be split. The on-chain blockchain only ever sees TWO transactions — OPEN and CLOSE — no matter how many payments happened in between.

txLifecycle.heading: The Life of a Bitcoin Transaction
txLifecycle.intro: Watch a single payment travel through every stage — wallet → mempool → mining → blockchain — until it's economically irreversible. Auto-plays; replay any time.

stages.title: Construct & Sign
stages.what: Wallet picks UTXOs as inputs, sets outputs (recipient + change), signs each input with your private key (ECDSA).

stages.title: Broadcast
stages.what: The signed raw transaction is pushed to your wallet's ~8 full-node peers.
stages.note: txid = double-SHA256 of the tx bytes.

stages.title: Relay & Validate
stages.what: Each node checks signatures, that inputs exist & are unspent, no double-spend, and fee sanity — then gossips it onward.
stages.note: Reaches ~50k nodes in under a second.

stages.title: Mempool
stages.what: Valid but unconfirmed. The tx waits in every node's mempool, ranked by fee rate (sat/vByte).

stages.title: Mining
stages.what: A miner selects the highest-fee txs, builds a candidate block (Merkle root), and grinds the nonce until blockHash < target.
stages.note: Proof of Work · ~10 min on average.

stages.title: Block Propagated
stages.what: The winning miner broadcasts the block. Every node re-verifies the PoW and all txs, then appends it to its local chain.

stages.title: Confirmations
stages.what: Each new block stacked on top buries it deeper. Rewriting it means out-hashing the whole network from this point on.

stages.title: Settled — Final
stages.what: The payment is economically irreversible. The recipient can safely release the goods or service.

architecture.heading: Bitcoin Architecture
architecture.subtitle: Four layers power the Bitcoin network — click any layer to explore it in detail.
architecture.emptyHint: ↑ Click a layer above to explore it

app.label: Application Layer
app.description: The user-facing layer. Wallets (MetaMask, Ledger), exchanges, and explorers translate raw blockchain data into readable interfaces. They communicate with the network via RPC calls to nodes.

p2p.label: Network Layer (P2P)
p2p.description: Nodes find each other using DNS seeds and a gossip protocol. Every unconfirmed transaction is broadcast peer-to-peer and held in each node's mempool until mined into a block.

consensus.label: Consensus Layer
consensus.sublabel: Proof of Work · Difficulty adjustment · Longest chain
consensus.description: Miners compete to find a nonce that makes the block hash start with enough leading zeros. Difficulty adjusts every 2,016 blocks (~2 weeks) to keep block time at ~10 minutes. The chain with the most accumulated work wins.

data.label: Data Layer
data.description: Blocks chain together via hash pointers. Each block contains a Merkle root of its transactions, enabling lightweight SPV proofs. The UTXO set is the minimal state needed to validate new transactions.

dataVisual.title: Block chain — hash linking
dataVisual.caption: Each block header references the previous hash — altering any block breaks all subsequent blocks.

steps.label: Peers
steps.desc: Each relays to their own peers

consensusVisual.title: Mining: find the nonce
consensusVisual.targetNote: Must start with enough leading zeros to meet current target difficulty

appVisual.title: Applications on Bitcoin
appVisual.caption: Apps communicate with the network via RPC calls to Bitcoin Core nodes (port 8332).

transaction.heading: Bitcoin Transaction & UTXO Model
transaction.subtitle: Unspent Transaction Outputs — the accounting primitive behind every bitcoin transfer.
transaction.whatB: is a discrete chunk of bitcoin that has been received but not yet spent. There are no "account balances" — your wallet software sums up all UTXOs locked to your address.
transaction.inputsBody: reference a previous UTXO by its transaction ID and output index (vout), and provide a scriptSig to prove ownership.
transaction.outputsBody: create new UTXOs — each locked to a recipient's public key hash. Once spent, a UTXO is consumed in full.
transaction.equation: Inputs (0.8 BTC) = Outputs (0.7 BTC) + Fee (0.1 BTC)

anatomy.desc: Protocol version — determines which rules apply (e.g. SegWit support).
Array of references to previous UTXOs being consumed. scriptSig (or witness) proves ownership.
Array of new UTXOs created. scriptPubKey locks the coins to the recipient's address.
Earliest time/block the transaction can be mined. 0 means no delay.

whyBenefits.label: No global balance
whyBenefits.desc: No single mutable account state — reduces contention

whyBenefits.label: Stateless validation
whyBenefits.desc: Each UTXO can be verified independently

whyBenefits.label: Parallel processing
whyBenefits.desc: Non-overlapping UTXOs can be validated concurrently

utxoExercise.heading: Exercise: Build a Bitcoin Transaction
utxoExercise.subtitle: Select UTXOs to cover the payment + fee. The wallet must spend entire UTXOs — excess becomes change.

scenarios.title: Simple payment
scenarios.context: Alice wants to send 0.4 BTC to Bob. Fee: 0.01 BTC. Which UTXOs should she use?
scenarios.explanation: UTXO B (0.5 BTC) alone covers 0.4 + 0.01 BTC. Change = 0.5 − 0.4 − 0.01 = 0.09 BTC back to Alice. Using only 1 input keeps fees low.

scenarios.title: Combining small UTXOs
scenarios.context: Alice wants to send 0.6 BTC to Carol. Fee: 0.01 BTC. No single UTXO covers it.
scenarios.explanation: UTXO A + B = 0.75 BTC. Covers 0.6 + 0.01 BTC. Change = 0.75 − 0.6 − 0.01 = 0.14 BTC back to Alice. UTXO C is not needed.

pow.heading: Proof of Work
pow.subtitle: The cryptographic puzzle that makes Bitcoin's consensus tamper-proof.
pow.puzzleB: (applied twice) to produce a 256-bit digest. Miners must find a
pow.puzzleC: such that the resulting block hash is below the current target — meaning it starts with enough leading zeros. This requires brute-force trial and error.

hardware.title: Mining Hardware Evolution
hardware.note: Each generation ~1000× more efficient. ASICs now dominate.

reward.title: Block Reward & Halvings
reward.note: Halving every 210,000 blocks. Supply hard-capped at 21 million BTC.

energy.title: The Energy Debate
energy.bodyA: PoW converts electricity into security — attacking is expensive in the real world. Critics note Bitcoin's energy use (~150 TWh/yr). Ethereum addressed this by moving to

attack.title: Why It Works: 51% Attack Cost
attack.body: To rewrite history an attacker needs >50% of global hashrate. At current difficulty that would cost billions in ASIC hardware + electricity, and any successful attack would destroy the value of the coins being stolen — making honesty the dominant strategy.

trilemma.heading: The Blockchain Trilemma
trilemma.subtitle: A blockchain can excel at any two of these — the third becomes a trade-off.

bitcoin.name: Bitcoin
bitcoin.detail: Every full node validates every transaction. Layer 2 (Lightning Network) handles micropayments off-chain.

ethereum.name: Ethereum + L2
ethereum.detail: Rollups (Arbitrum, Optimism, zkSync) batch thousands of L2 transactions into a single L1 proof — inheriting Ethereum's security.

solana.name: Solana
solana.detail: High hardware requirements concentrate the validator set. The network has experienced multiple outages.

apps.heading: Beyond payments — Bitcoin's app layer
apps.subtitle: For most of its life Bitcoin only did one thing: send value. Since 2018 — and accelerating since 2023 — a real application layer has emerged on, around, and atop the base chain.
apps.whereBodyA: Bitcoin's base chain handles ~7 TPS and has no smart-contract VM. Apps live in

items.title: Lightning Network
items.what: A network of payment channels. Two parties open a channel on-chain once, then exchange thousands of off-chain transactions instantly and almost free. Channels close on-chain to settle the final balance.
items.adoption: Apps: Strike · Cash App (US Bitcoin) · Phoenix · Wallet of Satoshi · Cashu · Public capacity ~5,000 BTC · ~15,000 routing nodes.
items.limit: Liquidity must be pre-allocated · routing failures common at the edge · custodial wallets dominate retail UX, eroding the self-sovereignty argument.

items.title: Ordinals · BRC-20 · Runes
items.what: Casey Rodarmor's Ordinals (Jan 2023) numbers each satoshi and lets you "inscribe" arbitrary data onto it — images, JSON, code. BRC-20 reused this for fungible tokens; Runes (April 2024) added a more efficient native-token system.
items.adoption: ~$4B+ Ordinals secondary market · BRC-20 momentum cooled in 2024 · Runes inherited the use case after the Halving.
items.limit: Highly contested: bloats blockchain data, raises base-chain fees during inscription waves. Many node operators and core devs oppose it philosophically — "Bitcoin is for money".

items.title: Bitcoin sidechains & rollups
items.what: Programmable layers pegged to BTC. Stacks (Clarity language, settles via "Proof of Transfer"); Liquid (federation, fast confidential txs); Rootstock (EVM-compatible, merge-mined). New entrants 2024-25: BitVM-style rollups, BOB, Bitlayer.
items.adoption: Stacks ~$1B TVL · Liquid powers confidential txs for major exchanges · Rootstock has steady DeFi activity.
items.limit: Trust models vary widely: Liquid is federated (trust the signer set); Stacks settles to BTC but security remains debated; Rootstock pegs use merge-mining. None inherits Bitcoin's security as natively as ETH rollups inherit L1.

items.title: Bitcoin staking — Babylon
items.what: Babylon lets BTC holders stake their coins to provide economic security to PoS chains — without bridging or wrapping. If a validator equivocates, their staked BTC is slashed via a Bitcoin-native script.
items.adoption: ~$5B+ BTC staked by late 2024 · backed by multiple PoS chains seeking shared security · mainnet launched 2024.
items.limit: New and unproven at scale · introduces complex Bitcoin script · creates BTC-denominated yield, breaking with the long-held "Bitcoin doesn't pay yield" tradition.

apps.caveatBody: Bitcoin's app layer is debated within the Bitcoin community. Purists want the base chain minimal ("digital gold"); builders push to use it as a settlement layer for richer apps. Be skeptical of hype on either side — both camps have legitimate concerns.

lightningWhy.heading: Lightning Network — Why It Matters
lightningWhy.subtitle: Bitcoin's base layer processes ~7 transactions per second and takes ~10 minutes to confirm. Lightning solves this without changing Bitcoin itself.

items.text: — national Bitcoin wallet (Chivo) runs on Lightning
— millions of users send Lightning payments
— pay per second for content or services
— tip 1 sat for an article or a tweet
— IoT devices paying each other autonomously

items.preA: A party trying to cheat with an old state gets their funds
HTLCs ensure intermediaries can route but

callout.title: Key Insight
callout.body: Lightning doesn't replace Bitcoin — it sits on top of it. The base layer provides the trust; Lightning provides the speed. Same model as the internet: TCP/IP underneath, HTTP on top.

bestFits.heading: Where Bitcoin Wins — The Use Cases It Actually Owns
bestFits.subtitle: Bitcoin's design choices (fixed supply, UTXO model, intentionally limited Script, PoW security) make it spectacularly good at a narrow set of jobs — and explicitly not the right tool for many others.

items.name: Store of Value / Digital Gold
items.why: Fixed 21M supply + 15+ years of uptime + PoW security make it the most credible scarce digital asset.
items.example: MicroStrategy holds 450k+ BTC as treasury · El Salvador national reserve · BlackRock IBIT ETF ($50B+ AUM).

items.name: Cross-border settlement
items.why: Censorship-resistant, no intermediary, available 24/7. Settles in minutes vs days for SWIFT.
items.example: Used in inflationary economies (Argentina, Venezuela, Turkey) as USD-adjacent settlement rails.

items.name: Micropayments via Lightning
items.why: L2 payment channels deliver instant, near-zero-fee payments without touching the base chain.
items.example: Strike, Cash App, Wallet of Satoshi · streaming sats per second for content (Fountain podcasts).

items.name: Strategic reserve asset
items.why: Verifiable scarcity + jurisdiction-neutral + carrier-grade infrastructure → attractive as a "neutral" reserve.
items.example: US Strategic Bitcoin Reserve (2025) · sovereign wealth fund allocations · corporate treasury policies.

bestFits.notFitBody: rich smart contracts (use Ethereum), high-throughput retail point-of-sale on the base layer (use Lightning or another chain), private enterprise consortia (use Fabric), on-chain identity / governance / DeFi composability.

worstFits.subtitle: The same design choices that make Bitcoin great at digital scarcity make it actively bad at other jobs. If you find yourself fighting the protocol, you're using the wrong chain.

items.name: Complex smart contracts / dApps
items.why: Bitcoin Script is intentionally not Turing-complete — no loops, no persistent state, no rich logic. Workarounds (Counterparty, RGB) hit the same ceiling.
items.alt: Use Ethereum (EVM) · Solana · Cosmos SDK · Starknet for any kind of programmable money or app.

items.name: High-throughput retail payments (on L1)
items.why: L1 sustains ~7 TPS with ~10-minute blocks. Even moderate adoption pushes fees into double digits; you cannot run a card-network on the base layer.
items.alt: Use Lightning (BTC L2) for sats-level payments · Solana / Base / Avalanche for stablecoin retail.

items.name: Private B2B consortia (healthcare, KYC, RWA)
items.why: Public on-chain visibility + no built-in identity = a non-starter for regulated data sharing between named organisations.
items.alt: Use Hyperledger Fabric (channels) · R3 Corda · permissioned Ethereum forks (Quorum).

items.name: DeFi / NFTs / DAOs
items.why: No native tokens, no on-chain identity, no programmable governance. Every "Bitcoin DeFi" project ends up requiring Ethereum or a federated sidechain to actually compose.
items.alt: Use Ethereum + L2s · Solana · Sui for DeFi composability and rich asset standards.

worstFits.rightToolBody: if the answer is "store of value", "neutral settlement layer", or "censorship resistance" — Bitcoin wins. For literally anything programmable, pick a different chain (and that's fine).

quiz.question: Alice has two UTXOs: 0.35 BTC and 0.40 BTC. She wants to send 0.60 BTC to Bob and pay a 0.01 BTC miner fee. What must happen in this transaction?
options: Use only the 0.40 BTC UTXO and ask Bob to cover the remaining 0.21 BTC himself.
Combine both UTXOs (0.75 BTC total), send 0.60 BTC to Bob, pay 0.01 BTC fee, and receive 0.14 BTC as change back to Alice's wallet.
Split the 0.40 BTC UTXO into two outputs of 0.20 BTC each to cover the payment over two transactions.
Wait for a new mining reward UTXO before making the transaction, since the individual UTXOs are too small.
quiz.explanation: Bitcoin's UTXO model requires consuming whole UTXOs as inputs. Alice needs 0.61 BTC total (0.60 + 0.01 fee) — neither UTXO alone covers this, so both must be combined. The transaction spends both UTXOs (0.35 + 0.40 = 0.75 BTC) as inputs, creates an output of 0.60 BTC to Bob, and creates a change output of 0.14 BTC back to Alice's wallet. The 0.01 BTC difference between inputs and outputs is implicitly claimed by the miner. This is why wallet software tracks your UTXO set rather than a single balance.

quiz2.question: A miner finds a block whose SHA-256 hash satisfies the difficulty target. Just before broadcasting, another miner publishes a different valid block at the same height. The network briefly has two competing chains. How is the conflict resolved?
options: Both blocks are kept side-by-side; honest nodes choose whichever arrived first and a coordinator transaction merges the histories within 10 minutes.

The fork is resolved by Nakamoto consensus: nodes follow the chain with the most cumulative proof-of-work. Whichever side first extends with another block wins; the other block is orphaned and its transactions return to the mempool.
The orphaned block is included in a future block as an "uncle" and the miner still earns a partial block reward, similar to Ethereum pre-Merge.
The two miners must broadcast a coordinated tie-breaker transaction signed by a 51% majority of the hash rate within the next epoch.
quiz2.explanation: Bitcoin uses Nakamoto consensus — the chain with the highest cumulative proof-of-work always wins. When two valid blocks are found at the same height, the network temporarily forks; whichever side first extends with another block becomes the canonical chain, and the orphaned block's transactions return to the mempool to be re-included later. Bitcoin has no uncle-block reward (that was Ethereum's GHOST protocol pre-Merge); a stale block earns the miner nothing. There is no coordinator transaction or signaling — propagation latency and probability decide forks naturally.

quiz3.question: Bitcoin's network hash rate doubles over six months as new miners deploy faster ASICs. What automatically keeps block intervals near the 10-minute target?
options: The block reward is halved early to discourage new miners from joining the network.

Every 2,016 blocks (~2 weeks) each node independently recalculates the difficulty target from how long the previous 2,016 blocks took. With double the hash rate, the target shrinks (more leading zeros required) so finding a valid block becomes proportionally harder.
Bitcoin nodes vote in real-time on a new difficulty using miner signaling encoded in coinbase transactions.
A timestamp rule rejects any block published less than 10 minutes after its parent, enforcing the cadence directly.
quiz3.explanation: Bitcoin's difficulty adjustment is automatic, trustless, and runs every 2,016 blocks (~2 weeks). Each node compares the actual time elapsed against the expected 14 days × 24 h × 6 blocks/h = 20,160 minutes and scales the target accordingly: faster blocks → harder target. Halvings happen every 210,000 blocks but are unrelated to hash-rate changes. There is no miner voting on difficulty and no minimum time between blocks — the target alone constrains them probabilistically.

takeaways.title: Section 01 — Key Takeaways
items: Bitcoin is a permissionless, public blockchain — anyone can participate
Its architecture combines UTXO transactions, Merkle trees, and linked block headers
Proof of Work makes attacks prohibitively expensive — security through real-world energy cost
The Blockchain Trilemma: decentralization, security, and scalability cannot all be maximised simultaneously
Bitcoin prioritises security and decentralization — scalability is handled by Layer 2 (Lightning)

summary.heading: Section Summary
summary.subtitle: Everything covered in this section — at a glance

cards.title: Application Layer
cards.summary: Wallets, exchanges, block explorers — communicate with the network via RPC calls to full nodes on port 8332

cards.title: P2P Network
cards.summary: ~50k reachable nodes · Gossip protocol · Mempool holds unconfirmed txs · TCP port 8333

cards.title: Proof of Work
cards.summary: SHA-256 hash puzzle · 2016-block difficulty adjustment · 10-minute block target · First to find nonce wins

cards.title: UTXO Model
cards.summary: Inputs reference past UTXOs · Outputs create new UTXOs · No global balance · Change is a new output

cards.title: Halving Schedule
cards.summary: 50 → 25 → 12.5 → 6.25 BTC per block · Max supply 21M · Next halving ~2028 · Last coin mined ~2140

cards.title: Trilemma Trade-off
cards.summary: Bitcoin: Security + Decentralization · Scalability handled by Lightning Network (Layer 2) off-chain payments
